source: .specs/agent-scenarios.md → Feature: Synchronous slash command (no-LLM local command)
notes: In ONE live pi REPL session: a real warm-up prompt drives the rollout session through working→ready, then a purely-local slash command (/session — 'Show session info and stats', verified live against pi 0.70.0) is issued. /session renders session stats (id, message counts, tokens, cost) directly in the TUI WITHOUT an LLM round-trip and writes NOTHING to the rollout transcript (verified live: transcript line count unchanged before/after /session — 5 lines both times, with the SAME single user+assistant-stop turn). The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a working transition during the slash window — the session stays ready continuously from the moment the warm-up turn settles through the slash command to teardown. WHY interactive (not headless `pi --print`): a slash command only exists inside the REPL; headless pi is single-shot. WHY a warm-up turn FIRST: pi creates its rollout-*.jsonl only after the first user message, so the driver's resolve_transcript needs a real turn to bind the UUID; the warm-up also supplies the legitimate working→ready activity to contrast against the slash command's silence. WHY two warm-up arcs in the phase chain: pi flushes one assistant turn as MULTIPLE transcript-activity bursts (a mid-turn assistant write, then the stopReason=stop write), so the daemon emits a brief working→ready, then a second working→ready ~2s later — both belonging to the SINGLE warm-up turn (the transcript still holds exactly one user + one assistant/stop line). The spec chains through both bursts (turn_start→turn_done→turn_start2→turn_done2) so the slash_window dwell anchors PAST the final warm-up settle; it is NOT asserting a multi-turn conversation — there is one turn, flushed in two bursts. WHY the 6s trailing sleep after the slash: it holds the recording open well past the classifier debounce so the post-slash ready dwell is observed cleanly and the daemon-kill doesn't race the final-state emission. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears), so session_birth — which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' — legitimately matches that pre-session (same shape as pi/session-start and pi/multi-turn-conversation). The warm-up arcs and the post-slash dwell must land on the UUID rollout session: turn_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording, which is necessarily the rollout session (the pre-session never goes working); every later phase pins to turn_start's session via same_session_as. KEY ASSERTION: the warm-up turn produces working→ready activity on the rollout session; the subsequent /session command produces NO additional working transition (state stays ready for ≥4s across the slash window) and NO waiting; a single process_exited on the rollout session; final state ready. HOW runs differ: warm-up assistant wording, per-turn token counts, session UUID, PID, and sub-second timing vary; pi may flush the single warm-up turn as one OR two working→ready bursts — the spec models the observed two-burst shape, and the load-bearing assertion is the silent ready dwell across the slash window regardless of warm-up burst count. Stable structurally: warm-up working→ready activity on one rollout session_id, a continuous ready dwell (≥4s) across the slash window with NO further working transition, no waiting, a single process_exited, and a final ready state.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of REPL launch
Warm-up prompt → working (binds the rollout session; the pre-session never enters working)
First warm-up activity burst settles → ready; state never entered waiting
Second activity burst of the SAME warm-up turn → working again (pi flushes one turn as multiple bursts)
Warm-up turn fully settles → ready; state never entered waiting
PID bound to the rollout session
Local /session command responds in the TUI with no model call — the session stays ready throughout; no → working transition is recorded for the slash command, and state never entered waiting
REPL torn down once after the slash window → a single process_exited on the rollout session; state immediately before teardown is ready
